skill.name: playwright-skill
skill.description: Runs custom Playwright browser automation for web testing, screenshots, forms, login flows, responsive checks, and local dev server validation.
skill.summary: Automates browser testing and web validation tasks with Playwright.
skill.author: lackeyjb

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, environment, and script execution indicators. Most markdown detections are documentation examples, but run.js confirms a high-risk executor that installs packages and runs user-provided JavaScript. No evidence found of prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration, so this is not blocked as malicious.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary JavaScript Execution by Design
high_findings.description: run.js accepts code from a file, inline arguments, or stdin, wraps it, writes it to a temporary file, and executes it with require(). This is expected for a browser automation executor, but it gives any generated or supplied script full Node.js capabilities.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executor directly runs caller-controlled JavaScript through a temporary module. The behavior is intentional, but the code path confirms real arbitrary code execution risk.

high_findings.title: Automatic Dependency Installation Uses Shell Commands
high_findings.description: The executor runs npm install and npx playwright install chromium when Playwright is missing. This can change the local environment and introduces supply-chain and network execution risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The child_process import and execSync calls are direct evidence. The commands are hardcoded, which lowers injection risk but not installation risk.

high_findings.title: Environment Headers Can Propagate Secrets to Browsed Sites
high_findings.description: The helper reads PW_HEADER_NAME, PW_HEADER_VALUE, and PW_EXTRA_HEADERS, then applies them to browser contexts. This is legitimate for testing, but sensitive values could be sent to every target site visited by the automation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variables are intentionally converted into HTTP headers. Risk depends on user configuration and target URL, so confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: Localhost Port Probing
medium_findings.description: detectDevServers sends HEAD requests to common localhost ports to discover running applications. This matches the skill purpose, but it is still local reconnaissance and should be disclosed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The function explicitly loops through common ports and makes local HTTP requests. The scope is localhost, which makes it moderate rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Temporary Executable Files Are Written Under the Skill Directory
medium_findings.description: run.js writes wrapped automation code into .temp-execution files in the skill directory and removes older files on later runs. This is functional, but it can leave executable artifacts after a failed or interrupted run.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write and cleanup behavior is direct. The impact depends on workspace permissions and whether cleanup runs successfully.

medium_findings.title: Browser Automation Can Submit Credentials and Reach External Sites
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes login, form submission, external link checking, and arbitrary URL navigation patterns. These are normal Playwright capabilities, but they can expose data if used on untrusted targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples show credential entry and network navigation. The risk is contextual because no malicious destination is hardcoded.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Trigger Static Shell Patterns
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are in documentation code blocks that show npm, npx, cd, and node usage. They are not executed unless a user follows the examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced markdown examples, not runtime code. They remain relevant because the examples instruct users to run commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Mostly Localhost or Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Static URL findings point to localhost test targets, example.com, and official Playwright documentation links. No suspicious external collection endpoint was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in examples and resource links. I found no evidence of a hidden exfiltration endpoint in these cited locations.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Paths Are Installation Instructions
low_findings.description: Hidden home directory detections refer to possible Claude skill installation paths. They are not code that reads secrets or hidden user files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths appear in setup documentation. The context shows install locations, not credential harvesting.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Appear to Be False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography hits occur in ordinary Playwright examples and descriptive markdown. No hashing, encryption, or certificate handling code was found in the reviewed runtime files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts do not implement cryptography. This supports dismissal of the static weak-algorithm pattern as lexical noise.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Module Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The executor calls require() on a generated temporary file containing caller-controlled code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a direct dynamic execution sink with user-controlled content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup path invokes npm and npx through execSync.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are literal strings, but they still execute package manager operations on the host.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-to-Network Header Injection
dangerous_patterns.description: Environment variable values can become HTTP headers for browser requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The implementation intentionally maps environment values into outgoing request headers.

content.value_statement: Manual browser checks are slow and easy to miss across screens and flows. This skill writes and runs Playwright automation for repeatable web testing.
actual_capabilities: Detects common localhost development servers before running browser tests.
Creates Playwright scripts for screenshots, forms, login flows, and link checks.
Runs scripts through a Node.js executor with Playwright helper functions.
Supports Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit through Playwright APIs.
Applies optional custom HTTP headers from environment variables.
Documents advanced Playwright patterns for network, mobile, and accessibility testing.
limitations: Runs arbitrary JavaScript, so it should only execute trusted automation scripts.
May install Playwright dependencies when they are missing.
Requires a working browser environment for headed mode.
Does not provide built-in policy controls for allowed target URLs.

use_cases.title: Check a Local Web App
use_cases.description: Detect a running dev server, open the app, capture screenshots, and verify key UI states.

use_cases.title: Validate Release Flows
use_cases.description: Automate login, form submission, redirects, and broken link checks before a release.

use_cases.title: Review Responsive Layouts
use_cases.description: Run the same page across desktop, tablet, and mobile viewports with screenshot evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Capture a Page Screenshot
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Playwright skill to open my local app, detect the dev server first, and capture a full-page screenshot.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright to test the contact form. Fill each field, submit it, and report whether the success message appears.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Responsive Views
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the homepage at desktop, tablet, and mobile sizes. Save screenshots and summarize layout issues you observe.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Login Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Playwright script for the login flow, use provided test credentials, verify the redirect, and capture failure evidence.

output_examples.input: Test the marketing page on my local dev server.
output_examples.output: Found a local server, opened the page, captured desktop and mobile screenshots, and reported visible layout problems.

output_examples.input: Check whether the login form redirects correctly.
output_examples.output: Filled the login form with test credentials, submitted it, confirmed the dashboard redirect, and noted the final URL.

output_examples.input: Find broken links on the documentation page.
output: Checked external links with HEAD requests.
Reported working and broken links with status details.

best_practices: Run only trusted Playwright scripts and review generated code before execution.
Use test accounts and avoid real credentials when automating login flows.
Keep target URLs explicit and prefer localhost or controlled staging systems.
anti_patterns: Do not run automation against third-party sites without permission.
Do not place secrets in custom headers unless the target is trusted.
Do not rely on fixed waits when Playwright selectors or load states are available.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser tasks with Playwright, including screenshots, forms, login checks, responsive tests, and link validation.

faq.question: Does it work with local development servers?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes helper logic to check common localhost ports before running a test.

faq.question: Can it run in visible browser mode?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation recommends visible mode by default unless headless mode is requested.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The executor runs user-provided JavaScript and can install dependencies, which gives scripts broad local system access.

faq.question: Does the audit confirm malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No. The audit found high-risk execution capabilities but no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
